verificationNote: Requirements constructed from IEC 62443, NIST SP 800-82, and NACSA Act 854 obligations. Not endorsed by any standards body — verify against authoritative sources before use in formal assessments.
name: Backup and Recovery
description: OT backup and recovery is fundamentally different from IT backup. Restoring a SCADA server is only step one — the system must then be synchronised with the physical plant state before it can resume control. Recovery planning must include operations engineering, not just IT.

requirements.name: OT configuration backup
requirements.description: Back up all OT system configurations, programs, and data that are required to restore OT functionality: PLC ladder logic, DCS configuration, HMI screens/tags, SCADA database, historian configuration, network device configurations.

technical.summary: Scheduled automated backup of all recoverable OT configuration. Offline/isolated backup copies. Include all components in backup scope.
actions: Define backup scope — every OT system must be included: PLC programs, DCS configuration, HMI application, SCADA database, historian configuration, network device configs, engineering tool databases
Automate backups where possible (scheduled SCADA database backup, historian backup)
For PLCs and RTUs: upload and save current program after any change; store in version-controlled repository
Store backup copies on isolated media (not connected to production OT network or internet)
Offsite or offline copy for disaster recovery (NAS at separate site, encrypted cloud backup for non-safety-critical configs)
Test backup restoration quarterly — a backup that cannot be restored is not a backup

governance.summary: Backup completion monitored. Failed backups escalated. Quarterly restore test.
actions: Backup status reported monthly to OT manager
Quarterly restore test documented
Annual backup scope review — add new assets, remove decommissioned assets

slMapping.sl2: Automated scheduled backup; offline copy; quarterly restore test
slMapping.sl3: Immutable backup storage; restore test includes process reconstitution steps; RTO defined
slMapping.sl4: Real-time backup for critical configs; warm standby; restore tested annually under simulated incident conditions

requirements.name: OT cyber incident recovery procedure
requirements.description: Document and test OT recovery procedures specific to cyber incidents — including how to safely restore control systems to a known-good state while the plant is running, and how to coordinate with operations engineering.

technical.summary: Cyber incident recovery procedure covers: isolated restoration environment, integrity verification before reconnection to OT network, synchronisation with plant state, step-by-step restoration with operations sign-off.
actions: Develop OT cyber incident recovery procedure covering: 1) Isolate affected system 2) Verify integrity of backup 3) Restore from verified backup in isolated environment 4) Test functionality before reconnecting 5) Coordinate with operations on plant state 6) Reconnect with enhanced monitoring 7) Verify normal operation
Include operations engineering in recovery procedure (they control the physical process)
Define Recovery Time Objective (RTO) per OT system based on operational criticality
Test recovery procedure annually — tabletop minimum; full recovery test preferred
Include NACSA s26 notification requirements in recovery procedure timeline

governance.summary: Recovery procedure approved by OT manager and operations manager. Annual test. Post-incident update.
actions: Annual recovery test with documented results
Post-incident review updates procedure
RTO targets reviewed against operational risk annually

evidenceItems: Operations engineering sign-off on recovery procedure

slMapping.sl3: Tested procedure with operations engineering; RTO defined and tested
slMapping.sl4: Hot/warm standby for critical systems; recovery tested under simulated cyber attack conditions

requirements.name: Backup integrity verification
requirements.description: OT backups must be cryptographically verifiable — a backup that has been silently corrupted or tampered with provides false assurance and will fail at the worst possible time. Hash-based integrity verification at backup creation and before restoration is mandatory for all OT system backups.

legal.summary: IEC 62443 SR-3.4 requires software and firmware integrity verification to detect unauthorised changes. SR-7.3 requires verified, tested backups. NACSA s18 requires measures appropriate to NCII sensitivity — a corrupted backup of a critical SCADA system directly undermines recovery capability.

technical.summary: Generate cryptographic hash (SHA-256 minimum) of all OT backup files at creation time. Store hash separately from backup. Verify hash before any restoration. Alert on hash mismatch — backup is treated as compromised.
actions: Generate SHA-256 hash of each OT backup file immediately after backup completion
Store hash records separately from the backup itself (separate system or access-controlled database)
Verify hash before any restoration attempt — abort and escalate if hash mismatch detected
Include hash verification in quarterly restore tests — document result
For PLC/DCS program backups: use the vendor tool's built-in checksum function where available; supplement with independent hash if vendor checksum is weak
For SIS program backups: store signed checksums in physical secure location in addition to electronic record — serves as tamper-evident register
Alert OT security team if backup hash verification fails — investigate as potential tampering event

governance.summary: Backup integrity verification procedure documented. Hash mismatches escalated to OT security manager. SIS checksum register maintained as controlled document.
actions: Hash verification included in quarterly restore test records
Hash mismatch incident procedure defined
SIS checksum register reviewed and validated after each SIS modification
Annual backup integrity audit — random sample verification across all backup targets

evidenceItems: Quarterly restore test records (including hash verification result)

slMapping.sl2: SHA-256 hashes generated and stored; verification on restoration
slMapping.sl3: Automated hash verification with alerting on mismatch; SIS signed checksums in secure storage
slMapping.sl4: Cryptographically signed backup manifests; immutable hash record; any modification triggers alert and investigation

requirements.name: RTO/RPO definition and testing for OT systems
requirements.description: Recovery Time Objective (RTO) and Recovery Point Objective (RPO) must be defined for every OT system based on operational consequence of loss — not IT SLA defaults. OT RTOs are often tighter than IT RTOs because process disruption has safety, environmental, and economic consequences. RTOs must be tested, not assumed.

legal.summary: IEC 62443 SR-7.4 requires that systems can be restored within an acceptable time frame following a disruption. NACSA s26 notification timelines (6 hours initial, 72 hours interim) require that OT operators can quickly determine impact scope and recovery status — which requires that RTOs are known and tracked in real time during an incident.
legal.owner: OT Manager / Operations Engineering / OT Security

technical.summary: Define RTO and RPO per OT system tier. Test RTO annually for critical systems. Include plant reconstitution time in RTO — OT recovery is not complete when the server is online, it is complete when the process is back under safe automated control.
actions: Define OT system tiers by operational criticality: Tier 1 (safety/primary control — RTO <4h), Tier 2 (secondary control/historian — RTO <24h), Tier 3 (reporting/ancillary — RTO <72h)
Assign RTO and RPO to every OT system in asset register based on operational consequence assessment
Include plant reconstitution time in RTO: Server restore time + OT-IT reconnection time + process synchronisation time + operator validation time + return to automated control = total RTO
Consult operations engineering when defining RTOs — they understand the process reconstitution constraints
Annual RTO test for all Tier 1 systems: restore from backup, including process synchronisation simulation, document actual vs target RTO
NACSA s26 operational implication: OT operators must be able to assess incident impact against RTOs within the 6-hour notification window — ensure incident assessment process is practised

governance.summary: RTOs approved by OT manager and operations manager. Annual test results reviewed against targets. RTO shortfalls trigger investment case for faster recovery options.
actions: RTO/RPO register per OT system, reviewed annually
Annual RTO test for Tier 1 systems with documented results vs target
RTO shortfall: formal risk acceptance or remediation plan within 30 days of test
Operations engineering sign-off on RTO targets — confirms process reconstitution time is realistic

evidenceItems: OT RTO/RPO register by system and tier
Operations engineering sign-off on RTO targets
Annual RTO test plan and results
RTO shortfall risk acceptance records or remediation plan

slMapping.sl2: RTO defined per system; tested annually; documented shortfalls
slMapping.sl3: RTO includes plant reconstitution; operations engineering validated; annual test with results documented
slMapping.sl4: Warm/hot standby for Tier 1 systems achieving sub-1-hour RTO; automated failover tested semi-annually
